observations.managementAction: The MEO raised the issue to the contractors, however, they are not amenable as posting of warranty security is an added expense on their part. Nevertheless, management will continue discussion with the contractors on the said issue. No monitoring reports prepared because the posting of warranty security was not enforced by Management.
observations.reason: Management was not able to enforce the posting of warranty security because contractors are not willing to comply. No monitoring report was prepared.

observations.recommendation: 1.8. We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant draw journal entries to recognize the completed infrastructure projects as PPE in the books of accounts and provide for the corresponding depreciation.

2.5. We recommended that Management ensure compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-008 and require the:
2.5.1. Municipal Engineer to provide the Accountant and the GSO with the cost of the lot component of the road projects; and
2.5.2. General Services Officer (GSO) to maintain and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

3.5. We recommended that Management expedite the transfer of title of the lot in Namat-ican, Poblacion in favor of the Municipality to ensure ownership and to protect the interest of the Municipality.

4.7. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw journal entries to adjust the affected accounts as follows:

4.7.1. Buildings 1,407,448.82
CIP - Buildings and Other Structures 1,407,448.82
To close the completed projects to the proper asset account

4.7.2. Prior Year Adjustment 68,343.83
Accumulated Depreciation-Buildings 68,343.83
To provide depreciation for the CIP reclassified to the Buildings account, and adjust the overstated accumulated depreciation of the portion of the project already closed to the Buildings account

4.8. We further recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Accountant ensure that payments of projects do not exceed contract costs, completed projects are properly reclassified and provided with correct depreciation to present fairly the PPE accounts in the financial statements.

5.12. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
5.12.1. Coordinate with the Provincial Accounting Office or the system administrator to correct the glitches or system error to deter abnormal balances in the PPE accounts;
5.12.2. Analyze carefully manual accounting entries prior to posting and immediately recheck accounts with abnormal balances;
5.12.3. Determine the existence, condition and location of the PPE items without cost. In case located, determine the fair value through appraisal, and compute for the correct depreciation. If non-existent, request for the de-recognition from the accounting records following the procedures outlined in COA Circular 2020-006.
5.12.4. Prepare journal entries to adjust the over-depreciated PPE items for a fair presentation in the financial statements with the following suggested entry:
Accumulated Depreciation 25,730.96
Prior Years Adjustment 25,730.96

6.6. We recommended and Management agreed to require the supply/property officer to prepare the IIRUP and submit a copy to the disposal committee for their evaluation and as basis for the recommendation to the Local Chief Executive of the appropriate mode of disposal.

6.7. We further recommended and Management agreed to dispose of the unserviceable properties in accordance with the Handbook on Property and Supply Management as prescribed by COA Circular No. 92-386 to prevent further deterioration of the stocked items and to derive income from sale thereof.

7.13. We recommended that Management:
a. comply with the provisions of LBC No. 130 and revert the ₱265,135.00 unexpended funds received from DILG to the National Treasury;
b. submit a written request for the extension of the implementation period of the Repair/Improvement of Badeo BHS; and
c. facilitate completion of the procurement of equipment under the Livelihood Enhancement project of the DOLE. Also, communicate with DOLE-RO and inquire about the discrepancy in the amount of fund assistance as stated in the MOA and the actual amount received.

7.14. We also recommended that henceforth, Management ensure compliance with the terms and provisions in MOAs with source agencies for the implementation of PPAs to avoid non-utilization and non-liquidation of fund transfers.

7.15. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize fund transfers from National Government Agencies intended for disaster risk reduction projects and activities as Trust Liabilities- DRRMF instead of Due to National Government Agencies.

8.5. We recommended and Management agreed to require the:
8.5.1. Municipal Development Council to prioritize projects with sufficient funds, thereby limiting the number of projects for implementation. Also, for the MDC to carefully evaluate feasibility of the projects, especially those that have not been started. If not viable, revert the funds to the unappropriated surplus for re-appropriation to other priority projects; and
8.5.2. Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Officer to strictly monitor the implementation of the identified development projects especially those from previous years’ budget, and ensure completion within the timetable set.

9.11. We recommended that Management maximize utilization of the LDRRMF and direct the LDRRM Officer to:
9.11.1. ensure timely implementation of identified programs/projects/activities to strengthen the Municipality’s capability to respond to disaster situations; and
9.11.2. facilitate the preparation of an LDRRMFIP in accordance with the guidelines provided in COA Circular No. 2012-002, to include a list of projects and activities to be charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years.

10.7. We recommended and Management agreed to require the BAC personnel to prepare a checklist of all required documents based on the RIRR, existing laws, rules and regulations including COA Circulars to serve as guide in the submission of contracts and supporting documents, to ensure completeness.

10.8. We further recommended and Management agreed to require the:
a. BAC TWG to submit immediately the required documents for evaluation/review by the audit team;
b. BAC Secretariat to properly mark/stamp the supporting documents with the month, date and year when these were received as required under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184; and
c. Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of supporting documents prior to processing of claims.

11.4. We recommended and Management agreed to require the BAC Technical Working Group to include evaluation of bidders’ ongoing government and private contracts in the post qualification to ensure that the contract would be awarded to the most responsive and qualified contractor.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
a) Require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the RPCI and other property records with the books of accounts and to make the necessary adjustments or corrections for a fair presentation of the accounts in the Financial Statements.
b) Direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
1. Prepare RISs for the issuance of inventory items to end users, summarize through the SSMI and submit the same to the Accounting Office supported with RISs on a weekly basis; and
2. Prepare and maintain stock cards for each item of inventory.
c) Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
1. Maintain Ledger Cards and to compute the inventory costs using the Moving Average Cost, or the unit cost from its latest purchase, if moving average cost per unit cannot be determined; and
2. Ensure that JEVs prepared pertaining to the recognition of expenses for each of the inventory items, are duly signed supported with RISs when recognizing expenses for each inventory item.
observations.managementAction: MAcco and MTO reconciled the property records with the books of accounts and made necessary adjustments. Issuances of inventories to end users were summarized through the SSMI supported with RISs on a weekly basis. The Property Officer-designate has been maintaining Stock Cards. The MAccO had been using the e-NGAS which automatically computes the unit cost of each inventory item. The MAcco prepared JEVs as supported by RIS duly signed by the end-users/recipients and SSMI when recognizing expenses for each inventory item.
observations.reason: Stock cards are prepared per department instead of per item of inventory. The Property Officer will prepare the Stock Cards per item of inventory in CY 2022.

observations.recommendation:

observations.reason: Despite management’s best efforts, various intervening factors deterred timely implementation of the projects. For instance, management hired new technical personnel and projects were limited in implementation; some projects remained unimplemented as of year-end.

19.2.2. Variation orders (VOs) exceeded the ten percent threshold.
19.2.2.1. The cost of VO issued by management to cover extra work/change in work items of some projects exceeded ten percent of the original contract cost contrary to Item 1, Section 1.1 of R.A No. 9184, details are presented as follows:
Project Original Change Final % of
contract order contract VO
1. Construction of 1,439,401.46 277,335.92 1,716,737.38 19.27%
Tonguey
Barangay
Health Station
2. Improvement 1,055,395.18 148,895.59 1,204,290.77 14.11%
along Sagpat-
Manawal access
road
19.2.2.2. As provided in the procurement law, variation order beyond ten percent of the original contract price should be covered by another contract to be bid out if the works are separable from the original contract except for exceptional cases where it is urgently necessary to complete the original scope of work where the cost of variation can go up to 20 percent of the original contract price, subject to the guidelines to be determined by the GPPB: Provided, however, that appropriate sanctions shall be imposed on the designer, consultant or official responsible for the original detained engineering design which failed to consider the VO beyond 10 percent [Sec. 1.4, Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA 9184].
19.2.2.3. It was further noted that the VOs lacked the pertinent documents supporting the issuances; to wit:
19.2.2.3.1. Notice coming from the contractor giving full and detailed particulars of any extra cost;
19.2.2.3.2. Justifications of the Municipality’s representative/Project Engineer for the necessity of such variation [Sec. 1.5 (a), Annex E, of the Revised IRR of RA 9184];
19.2.2.3.3. Report of the findings and recommendations, together with the supporting documents, of the technical staff who conducted an on-the-spot investigation to verify the need for additional work to the Head of the Procuring Entity [Sec. 1.5 (c), Annex E, of the Revised IRR of RA 9184]; and
19.2.2.3.4. Copy of contract covering the variation [Sec. 1.4, Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA 9184];
19.2.2.4. Documentary requirements prescribed by 9.1.1.2 of COA Circular 2012-001, dated November 14, 2012 such as:
19.2.2.4.1. Copy of the approved original plans indicating the affected portion(s) of the project;
19.2.2.4.2. Duly revised plans and specifications;
19.2.2.4.3. Copy of approved/revised PERT CPM Network Diagram; and
19.2.2.4.4. Additional Performance Security as the variation order exceeds 10 percent of the original contract cost.
19.2.2.5. The absence of the documentary requirements for the above transaction prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 as well as the non-compliance of the pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184 cited above precluded the Audit Team from conducting further evaluation of the validity, necessity and reasonableness of the VOs.

14.3. We recommended and management agreed for the Municipal
Accountant and designated LDRRM Officer to submit the Report on

observations.recommendation:

preparation of the RPCPPE and eventual reconciliation of
property records to ensure reliability of the account presented
in the financial statements; and

labels/stickers for easy identification to facilitate the physical
inventory taking.

statements.

journal entries to correct the balance of the Motor Vehicle account
presented in the financial statements:

Account Debit Credit
Government Equity 443,452.39
Accumulated Depreciation-Motor 56,547.61
Vehicles
Motor Vehicles 500,000.00

respective Accumulated Depreciation of the affected PPE account.

the Municipal Treasurer to:

property from the Property Officer for property previously
issued not covered by PAR or ICS;
5.5.2 Immediately prepare the PAR or ICS upon issuance of
property to the intended user and a copy thereof to be
attached to the disbursement voucher to form part of the
supporting documents of the requisitions made for reference
in post-audit; and

ready reference and for monitoring purposes.

enable the latter to take up the disbursements in the books of accounts
to preclude the issuance of a notice of disallowance.

balances of affected accounts; and

preparation of journal entries to present reliable and accurate
financial statements.

8.7 We recommended that the MAccO:

account, with an aging schedule of accounts prepared
periodically; and
8.7.2 Analyze the occurrence of the abnormal balances and effect
the necessary adjustment on the affected accounts.

cost of the Agricultural Produce for Distribution pending
submission of pertinent documents to support disposal of the
assets; and

issues, complaints, and some other reasons should not be
considered;
11.8.3 Formulate/develop specific strategies/solutions to effectively
implement development projects. Consider formulating a
timetable within which to start and complete the
unimplemented projects; and

Municipal Engineering Office.
